summary: Jeff Atwood argues that web application speed remains critically important despite the trend toward rich AJAX interfaces. He cites research showing that users abandon slow sites—Google found a half-second delay caused a 20% traffic drop, and Amazon discovered even 100ms delays hurt revenue. Drawing on classic human-computer interaction research from 1968, he outlines the psychological thresholds for user attention (0.1s, 1s, 10s) and argues the 10-second limit is now outdated. He concludes that developers should prioritize fast, functional layouts over flashy slow ones, and use techniques like progressive rendering when true speed isn't achievable.
key_points: AJAX-heavy web applications are reversing the speed gains of the early web, with loading screens becoming common again
Google's experiment showed users requested more search results but engagement dropped 20% when the extra half-second load time was added
Amazon found that even 100-millisecond delays caused measurable revenue losses
The classic 1968 response time thresholds (0.1s instantaneous, 1s uninterrupted flow, 10s attention limit) still apply, though the 10-second threshold may now be closer to 5 seconds
When actual speed isn't possible, perceived speed through progressive rendering and clever feedback can substitute
money_quotes: Half a second delay caused a 20% drop in traffic. Half a second delay killed user satisfaction.
Unless you make it load fast, nobody will stick around long enough to find out what you have to offer.
We tried delaying the page in increments of 100 milliseconds and found that even very small delays would result in substantial and costly drops in revenue.
What's more important? Getting flash after 5 seconds, or functional no-frills layouts in less than a second? Let's get our priorities straight.
Speed still matters. And remember, the perception of speed is just as important as actual speed. If you can't be fast, be clever.
My mind now boggles at the fact that I now see loading pages that last several seconds when visiting Web sites more and more these days.
1.0 second is about the limit for the user's flow of thought to stay uninterrupted, even though the user will notice the delay.
key_insight: User tolerance for delays is measured in fractions of a second, not seconds—even tiny speed improvements have outsized impact on engagement and revenue.
